crossLagR 0.1.0

LISS panel: personality ↔ political orientation

liss.Rmd

This vignette is an abbreviated partial replication of @bakker_reconsidering_2021, using the LISS panel data ship with crossLagR. We ask whether the “closed-personality” composite (low openness + high conscientiousness) leads right-leaning political orientation (left–right self-placement), or vice versa. See the user guide for the full chapter, which also covers each Big-Five trait, all five political items, and several alternative specifications.

Setup 

library(crossLagR)
library(lavaan)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

data(liss_long)

liss_long is in long form with one row per (respondent × wave). For the CLPM we need three waves of two observed variables in wide form. Bakker et al. use the May 2008, 2013, and 2019 personality waves. Here we use the same three waves and pair personality with the left–right item from the preceding December.

Construct the composites

The closed-personality composite is the mean of reverse-scored openness items plus conscientiousness items. We compute a simple per-wave mean ignoring the factor model (the book chapter fits a measurement model instead).

open_items <- c("rich_vocab", "dffclt_undrstnd", "vivid_imagin", "no_interest",
                "excellent_ideas", "poor_imagin", "quick_undrstnd",
                "dffclt_words", "reflecting", "full_of_ideas")

cons_items <- c("always_prepared", "leave_belongings", "pay_attention",
                "make_mess", "chores_done", "forget_to_put", "like_order",
                "shirk_duties", "follow_sched", "exacting")

# Items reverse-scored in the LISS Big-Five battery (5-pt scale).
open_rev <- c("dffclt_undrstnd", "no_interest", "poor_imagin",
              "dffclt_words")
cons_rev <- c("leave_belongings", "make_mess", "forget_to_put",
              "shirk_duties")

reverse5 <- function(x) 6 - x

liss <- liss_long |>
  mutate(across(all_of(open_rev), reverse5),
         across(all_of(cons_rev), reverse5)) |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    openness_high      = mean(c_across(all_of(open_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    conscientiousness  = mean(c_across(all_of(cons_items)), na.rm = TRUE),
    closed_personality = mean(c(6 - openness_high, conscientiousness),
                              na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  ungroup()

Reshape to wide (3 waves: 2008, 2013, 2019) 

target_waves <- c(8, 13, 19)

wide <- liss |>
  filter(wave %in% target_waves) |>
  mutate(wave_label = match(wave, target_waves)) |>
  select(case_id, wave_label, closed_personality, left_right) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from  = wave_label,
              values_from = c(closed_personality, left_right),
              names_glue  = "{.value}{wave_label}") |>
  transmute(
    x1 = closed_personality1, x2 = closed_personality2, x3 = closed_personality3,
    y1 = left_right1,         y2 = left_right2,         y3 = left_right3
  ) |>
  drop_na()

cat("Complete cases:", nrow(wide), "\n")

CLPM 

clpm_fit <- lavaan(estimateCLPM(waves = 3), data = wide)
summary(clpm_fit, standardized = TRUE)

RI-CLPM 

riclpm_fit <- lavaan(estimateRICLPM(waves = 3), data = wide)
summary(riclpm_fit, standardized = TRUE)

Reading the contrast

Following @bakker_reconsidering_2021, the CLPM cross-lags suggest a modest reciprocal coupling between closed personality and right-leaning self-placement. The RI-CLPM, which absorbs stable between-person trait differences into the random intercepts IxI_x and IyI_y, typically reports much smaller within-person cross-lags — consistent with the idea that the two constructs are correlated traits more than wave-to-wave drivers of one another.

For the per-trait breakdown (all Big-Five facets × five political items), alternative specifications, and a discussion of the substantive implications, see the book chapter.
